metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `gerrit-verify.yaml` as a required edit target alongside `test.yml`: the pip-audit job must be mirrored into `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `build-and-test` job (or dispatched from it) so it gates the Gerrit `Verified` vote, consistent with how all other `test.yml` checks are mirrored.
findings.finding: The plan names `test.yml` as the target for the pip-audit job but does not enumerate `gerrit-verify.yaml` as an unenumerated consumer that mirrors every job from `test.yml`. The `gerrit-verify.yaml` file comment explicitly states it 'runs the SAME checks as test.yml against the EXACT patchset' to cast the Gerrit `Verified` vote. A pip-audit job added only to `test.yml` would NOT gate Gerrit submissions, leaving the `Verified` vote incomplete and allowing dependency-vulnerable code to land on `main` via Gerrit without the new check.
findings.location: What section / 'Dependency-vuln scanning' bullet; Scope section '.github/workflows/*'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the pip-audit step to the `build-and-test` job in `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` in the same place it appears in `test.yml`, or add it as a separate job in `gerrit-verify.yaml` with `needs: [clear-vote]` so its failure aggregates into the `Verified` vote.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `permissions: security-events: write` (plus `contents: read`) to the CodeQL workflow job spec, and call this out explicitly in the plan's What section as a required auth precondition for SARIF upload.
findings.finding: The plan states the CodeQL workflow will 'upload results to code scanning' but omits the required `security-events: write` permission. Without it, the `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif` step will receive a 403 and the workflow will fail — this is a mandatory auth precondition for SARIF upload to GitHub Advanced Security.
findings.location: What — CodeQL SAST section; AC: '.github/workflows/codeql.yml runs CodeQL for Python and uploads results.'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the codeql.yml job definition, add:
```yaml
permissions:
  contents: read
  security-events: write
```
Also confirm in repo Settings → Actions → General → Workflow permissions that the default is not locked to 'read-only' in a way that blocks job-level overrides.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether CodeQL is intended as post-merge monitoring (update the 'preventative' language) or as a true pre-merge gate — if the latter, add a CodeQL step to `gerrit-verify.yaml` (the existing pre-merge CI path) and state a positive rationale for whichever placement is chosen.
findings.finding: The plan frames CodeQL SAST as a 'preventative' supply-chain control, but the proposed trigger — push to `main` — fires only after Gerrit has already merged and replicated the change. In this repo, all changes land on `main` exclusively via the Gerrit-gated path; by the time CodeQL sees the code, it is already merged. The plan provides no positive rationale for this post-merge-only placement, and the existing gerrit-verify.yaml already has the pre-merge checkout machinery (checkout-gerrit-change-action) where a truly preventative scan would run.
findings.location: What section — CodeQL SAST; AC line: '.github/workflows/codeql.yml runs CodeQL for Python and uploads results'
findings.suggested_fix: If monitoring-only is acceptable, change the plan's 'preventative' framing to 'continuous post-merge monitoring + scheduled scan'. If a blocking gate is desired, add a CodeQL step to the `gerrit-verify.yaml` `build-and-test` job (it already checks out the Gerrit patchset) — but note CodeQL adds ~3-5 minutes to verify latency and the team should decide whether that tradeoff is acceptable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `security-events: write` to the permissions block of the new `codeql.yml` workflow (required for SARIF upload to GitHub code scanning), consistent with the repo's explicit least-privilege permission pattern.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the CodeQL workflow uploads results to code scanning, but does not mention the `security-events: write` permission that GitHub requires for SARIF uploads. Every existing workflow in this repo uses explicit minimal permission declarations (e.g., `test.yml`: `permissions: contents: read`; `release.yml` publish job: `permissions: id-token: write`). Without explicitly granting `security-events: write` in the new codeql.yml, the SARIF upload step will fail with a permissions error.
findings.location: What section — CodeQL SAST; AC line: '.github/workflows/codeql.yml runs CodeQL for Python and uploads results'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to codeql.yml:
```yaml
permissions:
  contents: read
  security-events: write  # required to upload SARIF to GitHub code scanning
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief CodeQL triage/response section to `CONTRIBUTING.md` (or `docs/releasing.md`) explaining how to review alerts, how to suppress false positives via `// codeql[rule-id]` annotations, and who is responsible for clearing findings before a Gerrit change is merged.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a CodeQL SAST workflow that will produce GitHub code-scanning alerts, but neither the AC nor the doc targets (docs/releasing.md, CONTRIBUTING.md) include any guidance on how contributors should triage, respond to, or suppress CodeQL findings. This is a new operational pattern with no documented response workflow.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection 'Handling CodeQL Alerts' to CONTRIBUTING.md: explain that alerts appear in the GitHub Security tab, that confirmed false positives may be dismissed with a justification comment in the GitHub UI or annotated inline, and that real findings must be addressed via a Gerrit change before the next release.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (and implement) a `pip-audit.toml` or equivalent config file for ignore lists and severity thresholds so that tuning the scan policy doesn't require editing workflow YAML directly.
findings.finding: The pip-audit job's failure policy (which severity levels fail the build, and whether any known-safe vulnerabilities or dev-only dependency vulnerabilities can be ignored) is unspecified and will likely end up hardcoded or absent, making future tuning brittle.
findings.location: What section — 'Dependency-vuln scanning' / `.github/workflows/test.yml` or new workflow
findings.suggested_fix: Create a `pip-audit.toml` (pip-audit's native config file) or pass a `--config` path in the workflow; document its location in CONTRIBUTING.md so maintainers know where to add scoped ignores with justification comments.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the pip-audit/OSV job's behavior under network failure: add an explicit `--timeout` flag, a retry step (e.g., `retry-action` or shell loop), and document whether a DB-unreachable failure should be `continue-on-error: false` (hard block) or surfaced as a distinct advisory annotation rather than a workflow failure.
findings.finding: The pip-audit/OSV dependency-scan job has an unaddressed external network dependency: if the OSV vulnerability database is unreachable or slow, the scheduled CI job fails with no retry, timeout, or degraded-mode fallback specified. The plan does not state whether this job is `continue-on-error`, retried, or given a timeout, leaving the blast radius (CI noise, alert fatigue, or false-negative if silenced) undefined.
findings.location: ## What — 'Dependency-vuln scanning' bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria — second AC line
findings.suggested_fix: In `.github/workflows/` for the pip-audit job, add `timeout-minutes: 10` at the job level, run pip-audit with `pip-audit --timeout 60`, and add a brief shell retry (e.g., retry the step up to 2 times with `uses: nick-fields/retry@v3`). Document in the workflow comments that a network failure causes a hard job failure (not silent skip) so the failure mode is explicit and reviewable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a single scanning tool (pip-audit OR OSV-scanner/osv-scalibr) in both the What section and AC2 so no tool-choice decision is left to the executor.
findings.finding: The tool choice 'pip-audit (or OSV)' is left to the executor rather than decided in the plan, creating an ambiguity that forces a mid-execution design decision.
findings.location: What section, bullet 2: 'add a `pip-audit` (or OSV) CI job'; AC line 2: 'A `pip-audit`/OSV dependency-scan job'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pip-audit (or OSV)' with 'pip-audit' throughout (it is the more standard PyPI-native tool and requires no extra install in most Python CI setups).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete in-session proving command for AC4 — e.g., point to an existing release on PyPI and run `gh attestation verify` (or `pip install <pkg>==<ver> && pypi-attestations verify`) to confirm the already-published artifact carries attestations, OR document that this criterion is verified by inspecting a specific prior release URL on pypi.org/project/<pkg>/<ver>/attestations.
findings.finding: AC4 requires 'a released artifact is confirmed to expose attestations', which can only be verified against an actual PyPI release — a post-sprint-only event that cannot be exercised in a PR or in-session CI run.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'a released artifact is confirmed to expose attestations'
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC4 into two parts: (a) docs updated (in-session verifiable) and (b) attestation presence verified against the most recent existing release using `gh attestation verify oci://ghcr.io/... ` or the PyPI attestations endpoint — cite the specific release version to inspect so the check is deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the pass/fail semantics for the pip-audit job in AC2 (e.g., 'exits non-zero and fails CI on any CVSS ≥ medium finding, with an explicit ignore-list for known false positives').
findings.finding: AC2 does not state what a passing result means for the pip-audit job (e.g., exit-code 0 = zero known vulns, or a severity threshold, or an explicit allowlist), so the criterion is not fully observable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'A pip-audit/OSV dependency-scan job runs in CI as a check'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'job fails CI on any unfixed vulnerability with CVSS score ≥ 4.0; known acceptable findings documented in a pip-audit ignore file or inline comment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by checking pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish release history) that `release/v1` currently resolves to ≥ v1.12.0 (which defaulted attestations to `true`), and add an explicit `with: attestations: true` to release.yml's publish step so the behavior is pinned and auditable rather than dependent on a floating tag's default.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'attestations on by default' for `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1`, but the actual release.yml publish step (line 65) uses a bare `uses:` invocation with no `with:` block whatsoever — there is no `attestations: true` parameter present. The action uses a floating `release/v1` tag, so the specific version (and therefore whether attestations default to on) is unverifiable from the repo. If the pinned release/v1 commit predates the attestations-by-default change (added in v1.12.0, Oct 2024), no attestations are emitted at all, making the AC 'a released artifact is confirmed to expose attestations' unachievable without an explicit opt-in.
findings.location: What section / Acceptance Criteria: 'a released artifact is confirmed to expose attestations'; .github/workflows/release.yml line 65
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a `with:` block to the publish step in release.yml:
```yaml
      - name: Publish to PyPI
        uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1
        with:
          attestations: true
```
This makes the attestation behavior explicit and immune to future default-changes in the action.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` emits Sigstore attestations by default with `id-token: write` alone (no `with:` block needed), or whether an explicit `attestations: true` / `generate-provenance: true` parameter must be added to the release.yml publish step — resolve this before writing consumer-verification docs.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'attestations on by default' for `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` combined with `id-token: write`, but the existing release.yml uses a bare `uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` with no `with:` block. Whether the `release/v1` floating tag's current default already emits Sigstore attestations without an explicit `generate-provenance: true` or `attestations: true` parameter is unverified.
findings.location: What section / AC: 'verify the release publishes PyPI digital attestations (`pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` + `id-token: write`, attestations on by default)' + `.github/workflows/release.yml` line 65
findings.suggested_fix: Check the pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish changelog/README for the release/v1 line: as of v1.10+, attestations are emitted by default when `id-token: write` is present, but if the project's pinned release/v1 resolves to an older commit, add `with: attestations: true` (or `generate-provenance: true` depending on the action version) to the publish step explicitly rather than relying on the default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to either pip-audit or osv-scanner (not both) and document the choice with rationale — specifically addressing how the tool handles a lockfile-less pyproject.toml project and whether SARIF upload to GitHub code scanning is required.
findings.finding: The plan defers the tool choice between pip-audit and osv-scanner ('pip-audit (or OSV)') to the implementor without resolving which is appropriate for this project's dependency manifest shape. These tools have different integration patterns, network requirements, and lockfile expectations, and the project has no requirements.txt / lock file — only pyproject.toml.
findings.location: What section: 'Dependency-vuln scanning — add a `pip-audit` (or OSV) CI job' + AC: 'A `pip-audit`/OSV dependency-scan job runs in CI as a check'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
